did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The study utilized a deep-learning convolutional neural network (CNN) classifier to distinguish between pigmented basal cell carcinomas and malignant melanocytic tumours using hyperspectral imaging data. The CNN was trained on manually annotated hyperspectral images to perform lesion classification (2-class and 3-class) and pixel-wise classification.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The article is titled 'Hyperspectral Imaging Reveals Spectral Differences and Can Distinguish Malignant Melanoma from Pigmented Basal Cell Carcinomas: A Pilot Study' and is labeled as an 'INVESTIGATIVE REPORT'. It describes original experimental work involving the recruitment of 26 patients, hyperspectral imaging data acquisition, manual annotation, and training/testing of a convolutional neural network (CNN) classifier. The paper reports specific results (sensitivity, specificity, PPV) from this primary data analysis, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Hyperspectral Imaging Reveals Spectral Differences and Can Distinguish Malignant Melanoma from Pigmented Basal Cell Carcinomas: A Pilot Study
brief_summary: This pilot study investigated the use of hyperspectral imaging (HSI) combined with a deep-learning convolutional neural network (CNN) to differentiate pigmented basal cell carcinomas from malignant melanocytic tumours. The researchers recruited 26 patients with histologically confirmed pigmented lesions, including pigmented BCCs, melanoma in situ, and invasive melanomas, and acquired in vivo hyperspectral images. A CNN classifier was trained on manually annotated hyperspectral data to perform both lesion-level and pixel-wise classification. The study evaluated the model's performance using sensitivity, specificity, and positive predictive value for 2-class and 3-class diagnostic tasks. Results showed that the CNN achieved 100% sensitivity and 90% specificity for distinguishing melanocytic tumours from pigmented BCCs at the lesion level. The authors concluded that HSI with CNN classification holds promise for automated melanoma diagnostics, though larger datasets are needed for confirmation.

lesion_types_studied.details: 10 lesions included in the study (6 nodular, 2 superficial, 2 both superficial and nodular). Used as a comparator class in 2-class and 3-class classification tasks.

12 lesions included in the study, all identified as lentigo maligna subtype. Combined with invasive melanoma into a 'melanocytic tumours' group for 2-class classification, and used as a distinct class in 3-class classification.

4 lesions included in the study (3 lentigo maligna subtype, 1 superficial spreading subtype). Combined with melanoma in situ into a 'melanocytic tumours' group for 2-class classification, and used as a distinct class in 3-class classification.
An aggregated class comprising melanoma in situ (n=12) and invasive melanoma (n=4), used as the positive class in the 2-class classification task against pigmented BCC.

all_task_types.target: Diagnostic category of skin lesions (pigmented basal cell carcinoma vs. melanocytic tumours) and specific lesion types (pigmented BCC, melanoma in situ, malignant melanoma).
all_task_types.details: The study employed a convolutional neural network (CNN) classifier to perform two-level classification tasks: a 2-class task distinguishing melanocytic tumours from pigmented basal cell carcinomas, and a 3-class task distinguishing pigmented BCC, melanoma in situ (MIS), and malignant melanoma (MM). The model predicted discrete diagnostic labels for whole lesions (using majority pixel voting) and for individual pixels (pixel-wise classification). Performance was evaluated using sensitivity, specificity, and positive predictive value.

datasets_used.name: Päijät-Häme Central Hospital Hyperspectral Imaging Cohort
datasets_used.description: A prospective pilot study cohort of 26 histologically confirmed pigmented skin lesions (10 pigmented basal cell carcinomas, 12 melanomas in situ, 4 invasive melanomas) from 24 patients recruited at Päijät-Häme Central Hospital, Lahti, Finland, between May 2014 and November 2017. In vivo hyperspectral images were acquired using a VTT FPI VIS-VNIR Spectral Camera prototype.
classes_used: Melanocytic Tumours (aggregated class of MIS and MM)

use.training: Half of each hyperspectral image was used for training the CNN. Approximately 27,000 data-points per class were sampled from annotated areas, augmented to create a final training set of approximately 655,000 data-points.
use.testing: The other half of each hyperspectral image was used independently for testing/classification to ensure no data leakage from the training set. Lesion-level classification was performed using majority pixel voting.
use.other: Pixel-wise classification was performed on the remaining half of the images, resulting in 164,144 classified pixels for evaluation.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To estimate the uncertainty of sensitivity, specificity, and positive predictive value metrics for lesion-level and pixel-wise classification tasks.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests). Statistical uncertainty was quantified using 95% confidence intervals for sensitivity, specificity, and positive predictive value, but no comparative hypothesis tests were performed or reported.

experiment_2_class_lesion_level.description: Classification of melanocytic tumours vs pigmented basal cell carcinomas using majority of pixels method for whole lesion prediction
experiment_2_class_lesion_level.notes: Only one true pigmented BCC was falsely predicted as a melanocytic tumour. All melanocytic tumours were correctly predicted.

experiment_2_class_pixel_level.description: Pixel-wise classification differentiating class 'melanocytic tumours' from class 'pigmented BCC'
experiment_2_class_pixel_level.notes: Total of 164,144 classified pixels. Confusion matrices shown in Fig. 5.

experiment_3_class_lesion_level.description: Classification of pigmented BCC (n=10), MIS (n=12), and MM (n=4) using majority of pixels method
experiment_3_class_lesion_level.notes: Calculated sensitivity, specificity, and PPV for distinguishing MM from other lesions (MIS and pigmented BCC). Confusion matrices shown in Fig. 5.

experiment_3_class_pixel_level.description: Pixel-wise classification between tumour classes only for class 'MM'
experiment_3_class_pixel_level.notes: Confusion matrices for 3-class lesion classification and 5-class pixel-wise classification shown in Fig. 5. Note: Paper text references Fig. 4 for these matrices, but Fig. 5 caption describes them.

architecture_used.input_size: Raw data cube size 240 × 320 × 66 pixels; 5×5-pixel neighbourhoods selected for training

architecture_details.operation_mode: Layers operated separately and simultaneously in both spatial and spectral domains
architecture_details.hardware: IBM PowerAI Platform with 2 Nvidia Tesla V100-SXM2 16 GB graphics processing units (GPUs)

training_details.data_augmentation: Mirroring and flipping horizontally and vertically
training_details.training_set_size: Approximately 655,000 data-points (derived from ~27,000 data-points per class)

classification_or_output_head.decision_method: Maximum likelihood (highest value of each pixel in likelihood maps) used to obtain final class classification per pixel
classification_or_output_head.lesion_level_aggregation: Majority of pixels method used to predict the class of the whole lesion

preprocessing_techniques: Radiometric calibration to reflectance using Saari et al.'s method with a white reference target (R = I / I0)
Spectral domain subtraction of average spectrum to reduce vignetting and lighting irregularities
Selection of 66 wavebands from the original 76 wavebands (spectral range 500–850 nm)
Manual annotation of histologically verified tumour, healthy skin, and marker pen areas by a clinician
Extraction of 5×5-pixel neighbourhoods from annotated areas for training data points
Data augmentation via mirroring and flipping horizontally and vertically

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Each training cube was mirrored and flipped horizontally and vertically to quadruple the number of inputs in the training phase.

conclusions: This is the first study to utilize hyperspectral images and a deep-learning convolutional neural network to acquire an automated diagnosis for melanocytic tumours and pigmented basal cell carcinomas.

The convolutional neural network classifier can differentiate melanocytic tumours from pigmented basal cell carcinomas in hyperspectral images with high accuracy (100% sensitivity and 90% specificity for lesion-level classification).
The results indicate that with a larger sample and training dataset, the convolutional neural network could accurately classify malignant melanocytic tumours from pigmented basal cell carcinomas.
This finding may serve as the basis for the development of future techniques in melanoma diagnostics.
Future studies are warranted to confirm these preliminary results using larger samples, multiple tumour types, and including all types of melanocytic lesions.
Development of future techniques requires a hyperspectral camera to be commercially available to clinicians.
important_remarks: The study is a pilot study with a very small sample size (26 lesions from 24 patients), which limits statistical power and generalizability.
The dataset exhibits significant class imbalance, particularly for invasive melanoma (n=4) compared to melanoma in situ (n=12) and pigmented BCC (n=10).
All melanoma in situ cases were of the lentigo maligna subtype, and invasive melanomas were mostly lentigo maligna melanoma, limiting the diversity of melanocytic lesion types studied.

The study population is highly selected: mean age was 73.1 years, predominantly Fitzpatrick skin phototypes II and III, and included patients with immunosuppression or history of other malignancies, reducing external validity for broader populations.

Data leakage was mitigated by splitting each hyperspectral image vertically into two halves: one for training and the other independent half for testing, ensuring no pixel overlap between training and test sets.
Six lesions were excluded from the initial 32 due to imaging artefacts (underexposure or FPI switch issues), which may introduce selection bias.
The CNN achieved 100% sensitivity for invasive melanoma in the 3-class lesion-level task, but the 95% confidence interval was extremely wide (51–100%) due to the small number of positive cases (n=4).

One pigmented BCC was falsely classified as a melanocytic tumour; the authors note this may be due to entrapped melanocytes or pigment macrophages histologically present in the BCC, representing a biological rather than purely technical limitation.
The study relied on a prototype hyperspectral camera (VTT FPI VIS-VNIR), which is not commercially available to clinicians, highlighting a barrier to clinical deployment.
The imaging depth of the HSI system is approximately 2 mm, which may limit the assessment of deeper dermal structures depending on melanin concentration.
No formal hypothesis testing (p-values) was performed; statistical uncertainty was reported only via 95% confidence intervals.
The study did not include benign pigmented lesions (e.g., seborrheic keratosis, dermatofibroma) in the classification tasks, limiting the assessment of specificity against common benign mimics.
